Summary: In metastatic melanoma, PD-1 is expressed on T cells and PD-L1 is expressed on tumor cells and on tumor-derived exosomes. Tumor cell surface PD-L1 interacts with PD-1 on T cells, and extracellular PD-L1 on released exosomes binds PD-1 on antigen-experienced CD8 T cells. Binding of tumor cell surface PD-L1 to PD-1 inactivates T cells. Exosomal PD-L1 binds PD-1 in a concentration-dependent manner, and IFN-γ treatment increases exosomal PD-L1 levels and correspondingly increases exosome binding to PD-1. Exosomal PD-L1 suppresses CD8 T cell function, facilitates tumor growth, and enables tumors to counteract immune pressure at the effector stage. Anti-PD-L1 or PD-1 antibodies block the exosomal PD-L1–PD-1 interaction. In patients, circulating exosomal PD-L1 positively correlates with IFN-γ and changes during anti-PD-1 therapy; early on-treatment increases stratify clinical responders from non-responders and support its use as a predictor for anti-PD-1 therapy.
References: Chen, G., Huang, A. C., Zhang, W., Zhang, G., Wu, M., Xu, W., Yu, Z., Yang, J., Wang, B., Sun, H., Xia, H., Man, Q., Zhong, W., Antelo, L. F., Wu, B., Xiong, X., Liu, X., Guan, L., Li, T., … Guo, W. (2018). Exosomal PD-L1 contributes to immunosuppression and is associated with anti-PD-1 response. Nature, 560(7718), 382-386. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-018-0392-8
